content:
# Exodus Chapter 39 — The PNEUMA Bible (Dynamic)
**Translator:** PNEUMA | **Source:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), pp. 110–112; Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209), pp. 82–83 | **Source Images:** `source-texts/hebrew-OT/EXO_039_page_001.jpg`, `source-texts/hebrew-OT/EXO_039_page_002.jpg`, `source-texts/hebrew-OT/EXO_039_page_003.jpg`, `source-texts/greek-OT-LXX/EXO_039_vaticanus_page_001.jpg`, `source-texts/greek-OT-LXX/EXO_039_vaticanus_page_002.jpg`

---

### The Woven Fabrics for Priestly Service (v. 1)

**1** From the blue, purple, and scarlet yarn they made finely woven garments for ministry in the sanctuary. They also made the sacred garments for Aaron — exactly as YHWH had commanded Moses.

---

### The Ephod (vv. 2–7)

**2** They made the ephod out of gold thread, blue, purple, and scarlet yarn, and finely woven linen. **3** They hammered gold sheets thin, then cut them into threads to be worked in among the blue, purple, and scarlet yarn and the fine linen — the work of a master craftsman. **4** They made shoulder pieces for it, joined at its two upper corners. **5** The skillfully woven waistband of the ephod was made of one piece with it, of the same workmanship — gold thread, blue, purple, and scarlet yarn, and finely woven linen — exactly as YHWH had commanded Moses.

**6** They prepared the onyx stones, mounted in gold filigree settings, and engraved on them the names of the sons of Israel, as a gem-cutter engraves a seal. **7** He set them on the shoulder pieces of the ephod as memorial stones for the Sons of Israel — exactly as YHWH had commanded Moses.

---

### The Breastpiece (vv. 8–21)

**8** They made the breastpiece — the work of a master craftsman, matching the ephod — out of gold thread, blue, purple, and scarlet yarn, and finely woven linen. **9** It was square when folded double, a span long and a span wide. **10** They mounted four rows of precious stones on it. The first row held a ruby, a topaz, and an emerald. **11** The second row held a turquoise, a sapphire, and a diamond. **12** The third row held a jacinth, an agate, and an amethyst. **13** The fourth row held a beryl, an onyx, and a jasper — all set in gold filigree mountings. **14** There were twelve stones in all, one for each of the names of the sons of Israel, each engraved like a seal with the name of one of the twelve tribes.

**15** For the breastpiece they made braided chains of pure gold, twisted like cords. **16** They made two gold filigree settings and two gold rings, and fastened the two rings to the two upper corners of the breastpiece. **17** They attached the two gold cords to the two rings at the corners of the breastpiece. **18** The other ends of the two cords they attached to the two filigree settings, and then fastened them to the front of the ephod's shoulder pieces. **19** They made two more gold rings and attached them to the two lower corners of the breastpiece, on the inside edge next to the ephod. **20** They made two more gold rings and attached them to the bottom of the ephod's shoulder pieces, on the front, close to the seam just above the waistband of the ephod. **21** They tied the breastpiece by its rings to the rings of the ephod with a cord of blue yarn, so that it rested securely on the ephod's waistband and would not come loose from it — exactly as YHWH had commanded Moses.

---

### The Robe of the Ephod (vv. 22–26)

**22** They made the robe that goes under the ephod entirely of blue yarn — the work of a weaver. **23** There was an opening in the center of the robe, like the collar of a coat of armor, with a reinforced binding around the opening so it would not tear. **24** Along the hem of the robe they made pomegranates of blue, purple, and scarlet yarn and finely woven linen. **25** They also made bells of pure gold and placed the bells between the pomegranates all around the hem of the robe — **26** a bell, then a pomegranate, a bell, then a pomegranate, all around the hem of the robe for ministry — exactly as YHWH had commanded Moses.

---

### The Tunics, Turban, and Undergarments (vv. 27–29)

**27** They made the tunics of fine linen — the work of a weaver — for Aaron and his sons. **28** They also made the turban of fine linen, the ornamental headbands of fine linen, and the linen undergarments of finely woven linen. **29** The sash was made of finely woven linen with blue, purple, and scarlet yarn — the work of an embroiderer — exactly as YHWH had commanded Moses.

---

### The Gold Plate of the Sacred Crown (vv. 30–31)

**30** They made the plate for the sacred crown out of pure gold and inscribed on it, as one engraves a seal: "Holy to YHWH." **31** They fastened a cord of blue yarn to it in order to attach it to the front of the turban — exactly as YHWH had commanded Moses.

---

### The Work Completed and Brought to Moses (vv. 32–43)

**32** So all the work on the tabernacle — the tent of meeting — was finished. The Sons of Israel had done everything exactly as YHWH had commanded Moses. That is just what they did.

**33** Then they brought the tabernacle to Moses: the tent with all its equipment — its clasps, frames, crossbars, posts, and bases; **34** the covering of ram skins dyed red, the covering of fine leather, and the shielding curtain; **35** the ark of the covenant law with its poles and the mercy seat; **36** the table with all its utensils and the bread of the Presence; **37** the pure gold lampstand with its lamps set in place, all its utensils, and the oil for the light; **38** the gold altar, the anointing oil, the fragrant incense, and the screen for the entrance to the tent; **39** the bronze altar with its bronze grating, its poles, and all its utensils; the basin and its stand; **40** the hangings for the courtyard with its posts and bases, the screen for the courtyard entrance, its ropes and tent pegs, and all the equipment for the service of the tabernacle — the tent of meeting; **41** and the finely woven garments for ministry in the sanctuary — the sacred garments for Aaron the priest and the garments for his sons to serve as priests.

**42** The Sons of Israel had done all the work exactly as YHWH had commanded Moses. **43** Moses inspected all the work, and indeed, they had done it just as YHWH had commanded. That is exactly how they had made it. And Moses blessed them.

---

## PNEUMA's Meaning Notes

### Verse 1 — The woven service garments
The opening verse distinguishes between two categories: the בִּגְדֵי הַשְּׂרָד (bigdei haserad, "service garments" or "woven garments") used for ministry in the sanctuary, and the בִּגְדֵי הַקֹּדֶשׁ (bigdei haqqodesh, "sacred garments") specifically for Aaron. The term שְׂרָד (serad) appears only in connection with the tabernacle and is related to weaving or plaiting; it likely refers to the richly woven fabric used in sanctuary service. The LXX renders this with a reference to λειτουργεῖν (leitourgein, "to minister/serve"), from which English gets "liturgy" — public sacred service. The dynamic rendering "finely woven garments for ministry" captures both the material quality and the functional purpose. The refrain כַּאֲשֶׁר צִוָּה יְהוָה אֶת־מֹשֶׁה (ka'asher tsivvah YHWH et-Mosheh, "as YHWH commanded Moses") appears seven times in this chapter (vv. 1, 5, 7, 21, 26, 29, 31) for the garment construction, then echoes in the completion summary (vv. 32, 42, 43) — a drumbeat of obedience that structures the entire chapter.

### Verses 2–7 — The ephod and its craftsmanship
The אֵפוֹד (ephod) was the outermost garment of the high priest, a kind of apron or vest worn over the robe. The extraordinary detail about hammering gold into sheets and then cutting those sheets into threads (v. 3) — וַיְרַקְּעוּ אֶת־פַּחֵי הַזָּהָב וְקִצֵּץ פְּתִילִם (vayeraqqe'u et-pachei hazzahav veqitsets petilim) — describes a technique of astonishing skill: gold leaf thin enough to be sliced into wire-like threads and woven among colored yarns. This was genuine goldwork textile, not mere decoration. The onyx stones (אַבְנֵי שֹׁהַם, avnei shoham) on the shoulder pieces bore the names of the twelve sons of Israel, making the high priest literally carry the nation on his shoulders before God. The term אַבְנֵי זִכָּרוֹן (avnei zikkaron, "stones of remembrance/memorial") in v. 7 conveys that these stones served a theological function: they brought Israel to God's attention, reminding God of His covenant people. The LXX uses λίθους μνημοσύνου (lithous mnemosynou, "stones of remembrance"), preserving this memorial function. The Vaticanus text on p. 82 preserves this passage with the characteristic Greek rendering of the ephod construction.

### Verses 8–21 — The breastpiece of judgment
The חֹשֶׁן (choshen, "breastpiece") is sometimes called the חֹשֶׁן הַמִּשְׁפָּט (choshen hammishpat, "breastpiece of judgment"), though this fuller title appears in the instructions (28:15) rather than here in the construction account. It was folded double to form a square pocket — a זֶרֶת (zeret, "span," roughly 9 inches / 23 cm) on each side. The twelve stones remain difficult to identify with certainty; ancient gem nomenclature does not map neatly onto modern mineralogy. The translation follows traditional identifications while acknowledging scholarly uncertainty. The Hebrew אֹדֶם (odem, "ruby/carnelian"), פִּטְדָה (pitdah, "topaz/chrysolite"), בָּרֶקֶת (bareqet, "emerald/beryl"), and so on represent a range of colored precious and semi-precious stones. Each stone bore one tribal name, so the high priest carried all twelve tribes over his heart when he entered God's presence — the shoulders bearing them (ephod) and the heart embracing them (breastpiece). The elaborate attachment system (vv. 15–21) ensured the breastpiece could never separate from the ephod: gold chains above, a blue cord binding below. The dynamic rendering emphasizes the functional logic of this system — the breastpiece had to stay securely in place because it represented the unbreakable bond between the priest and the people he represented. The LXX in Vaticanus describes the breastpiece construction (τὸ λογεῖον, to logeion, "the oracle-piece") with attention to the same attachment details.

### Verses 22–26 — The robe and its bells and pomegranates
The מְעִיל (me'il, "robe") was entirely of blue (תְּכֵלֶת, tekhelet) — the color associated with the heavens and with royalty. The comparison of its collar opening to a תַּחְרָא (tachra, "coat of armor" or "leather corslet") indicates a reinforced, woven edge that would not fray or tear — practically important, since 28:32 warns that the robe must not be torn. The alternating bells (פַּעֲמֹנֵי זָהָב, pa'amonei zahav, "bells of gold") and pomegranates (רִמּוֹנִים, rimmonim) around the hem created a visible and audible testimony: the pomegranate, a symbol of fruitfulness and abundance in the ancient Near East, paired with the clear sound of gold bells. The priest was to be heard approaching God and leaving God's presence (28:35). The dynamic rendering "for ministry" captures the Hebrew לְשָׁרֵת (lesharet, "to minister/serve"), indicating the robe's liturgical purpose. The LXX renders the pomegranates with ῥοΐσκους (rhoiskous, "small pomegranates") and the bells with κώδωνας (kodonас, "bells"), preserving the alternating pattern.

### Verses 27–29 — The undergarments
The כָּתְנוֹת (kotnot, "tunics") were the base layer, worn directly on the body — the same word used for the garments God made for Adam and Eve in Genesis 3:21. They were made of שֵׁשׁ (shesh, "fine linen"), as were the מִגְבָּעוֹת (migba'ot, "headbands/caps") for the ordinary priests and the מִכְנְסֵי־בָד (mikhnsei-vad, "linen undergarments/breeches"). The אַבְנֵט (avnet, "sash") was the one piece that combined the fine linen with the colored yarns (blue, purple, scarlet), adding a note of splendor to the otherwise plain linen undergarments. The distinction between Aaron's מִצְנֶפֶת (mitsnefet, "turban") and his sons' מִגְבָּעוֹת (migba'ot, "headbands") marked the hierarchical difference between the high priest and the ordinary priests. The LXX renders the turban as μίτρα (mitra), from which English derives "mitre" — the traditional term for a bishop's headpiece, showing the long legacy of this priestly vestment language.

### Verses 30–31 — "Holy to YHWH"
The צִּיץ (tsits, "plate" or "blossom/flower") was a plate of pure gold affixed to the front of the turban by a blue cord. The word tsits literally means "blossom" or "shining thing" — the same word used for flowers in Isaiah 40:6–8 ("the grass withers, the flower fades"). Engraved on it like a seal were the words קֹדֶשׁ לַיהוָה (qodesh la-YHWH, "Holy to YHWH"). This was the crown of the priestly garments — worn on the forehead (מֵצַח, metsach), the most visible part of the body, declaring that the priest and the people he represented were consecrated entirely to YHWH. According to 28:38, this plate also served to bear the guilt of the sacred offerings, making them acceptable before God. The LXX renders it as πέταλον (petalon, "leaf/plate"), capturing the thin, flat shape. The Vaticanus text preserves ἁγίασμα κυρίου (hagiasma kyriou, "holy thing of the Lord") for the inscription. The dynamic rendering "Holy to YHWH" maintains the transliteration of the divine name consistent with the project conventions.

### Verses 32–43 — Completion, inspection, and blessing
These closing verses form the theological climax of the entire construction narrative (chapters 35–39). The phrase וַתֵּכֶל כָּל־עֲבֹדַת (vattekhel kol-avodat, "and all the work was completed") in v. 32 deliberately echoes Genesis 2:1–2, where God completed (וַיְכֻלּוּ, vayekhullu) the heavens and the earth. The tabernacle construction is presented as a new creation — a sacred space mirroring the cosmic order. The inventory in vv. 33–41 is a comprehensive catalogue of everything that was made, presented to Moses for inspection — a formal handover from builders to leader. The verb וַיַּרְא (vayyar', "and he saw") in v. 43, describing Moses inspecting the work, echoes וַיַּרְא אֱלֹהִים (vayyar' Elohim, "and God saw") from the creation account (Gen. 1:31) — Moses saw that everything was done correctly, just as God saw that everything was good. The blessing Moses gave — וַיְבָרֶךְ אֹתָם מֹשֶׁה (vayevarekh otam Mosheh, "and Moses blessed them") — mirrors God's blessing of the seventh day in Genesis 2:3. The sevenfold repetition of "as YHWH commanded Moses" throughout the garment construction (vv. 1–31), followed by the threefold repetition in the completion summary (vv. 32, 42, 43), yields a total that emphasizes complete, wholehearted obedience. The LXX in Vaticanus preserves this structure, with καθάπερ συνέταξεν κύριος τῷ Μωυσῇ (kathaper synetaxen kyrios to Mouyse, "just as the Lord commanded Moses") appearing throughout. The dynamic rendering "That is just what they did" and "That is exactly how they had made it" captures the emphatic Hebrew constructions כֵּן עָשׂוּ (ken asu) that punctuate the text — not mere compliance but joyful, precise fulfillment of the divine design.

### Vaticanus Divergences
The LXX in Codex Vaticanus (pp. 82–83) presents this material with notable structural differences from the MT. (1) The chapter heading visible on p. 83 reads ΚΕΦ. ΛΘΙΘ (Chapter 39), but the content arrangement differs from the MT. The LXX chapter 39 (= LXX 36 in some numbering systems) incorporates material the MT distributes differently — in particular, the Vaticanus text places the ephod, breastpiece, and garment construction in what corresponds to its chapter 36, while its chapter 39 contains material about the tabernacle erection that the MT places in chapter 40. (2) The order of the priestly garments in the LXX construction account differs from the MT: the LXX tends to describe the ephod and breastpiece construction before the other garments, matching the MT's order, but with variations in the level of detail for the attachment mechanisms. (3) The completion and inspection scene (MT 39:32–43) is present in the LXX but with some compression — the lengthy inventory list is preserved, but the LXX characteristically reduces some of the repetitive phrasing. (4) The creation-echo language at the chapter's conclusion — "all the work was completed," Moses "saw," Moses "blessed" — is preserved in the Greek, maintaining the theological parallel between tabernacle completion and cosmic creation. The Vaticanus scribal hand on p. 83 shows the text flowing continuously in its three-column uncial format, with the characteristic lack of word spacing that requires careful reading of the scriptio continua. (5) The refrain "as the Lord commanded Moses" appears in the LXX with the same structural emphasis as in the MT, though the Greek uses κύριος (kyrios, "Lord") where the MT has the tetragrammaton YHWH — reflecting the standard LXX practice of rendering the divine name as κύριος rather than transliterating it.
